title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt:
A health organisation has asked students to write a report about eating habits at their school. Your report should describe what students typically eat, explain whether these habits are healthy or not, and suggest ways to encourage better nutrition among young people.

Write your report.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Eating Habits at Northfield Academy

Introduction
This report examines the current eating habits of students at Northfield Academy and provides recommendations for promoting healthier nutrition.

Current Eating Habits
A questionnaire completed by 150 students revealed a worrying picture. Over 70% of students reported eating fast food at least three times a week, and fewer than 20% bring a home-prepared lunch to school. The school canteen offers a mix of healthy and unhealthy options, but the queue for pizza and chips is consistently longer than the one for salads.

Assessment
These habits are a concern. Diets high in fat, salt, and sugar are linked to obesity, poor concentration, and long-term health problems. During adolescence, when the body is still developing, good nutrition is especially important.

Recommendations
The school should consider replacing sugary drinks in vending machines with water and fruit juice. Cooking classes could teach students to prepare simple healthy meals, and the canteen could display nutritional information clearly beside each dish.

Conclusion
With targeted initiatives, the school can help students develop healthier eating habits that will benefit them throughout their lives.

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in a food and lifestyle magazine:

'We are looking for reviews of local restaurants or cafés that focus on healthy eating. Tell our readers about the food, the atmosphere, the price, and whether you would recommend it.'

Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Review: The Green Bowl — Healthy Food Without Compromise

For a long time, 'healthy eating' in restaurants meant bland salads and disappointing portions. The Green Bowl has changed my mind completely.

Located in a quiet side street just off the main shopping area, this café specialises in plant-based and whole-food dishes. The menu changes weekly to reflect seasonal ingredients, which keeps things interesting for regular visitors. I tried the roasted beetroot and quinoa bowl with tahini dressing, and it was genuinely delicious — colourful, filling, and full of flavour.

The atmosphere is warm and relaxed, with wooden furniture and plants creating a calming environment. It is the sort of place where you want to linger over a herbal tea rather than rushing off.

Prices are slightly above average, but portions are generous and the quality of ingredients clearly justifies the cost. Staff are friendly and happy to advise on dietary requirements.

For anyone who cares about what they eat but refuses to be bored by it, The Green Bowl is an outstanding find. I will certainly be returning — and soon.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt:
Your English-speaking classmate Morgan is writing a project about fast food and its effects on young people's health. Morgan has asked you to write an email sharing your views on fast food, explaining how often you eat it and why, and suggesting healthier alternatives.

Write your email to Morgan.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Hi Morgan,

Thanks for asking me to contribute to your project — I'm happy to share my thoughts!

Honestly, I eat fast food more often than I should, probably two or three times a week. I know it's not ideal, but when I finish school at 5 p.m. and still have homework to do, fast food is quick, cheap, and filling. I think that's exactly why it's so popular among young people — we're busy and on a budget.

Of course, the downsides are well-known. Fast food tends to be high in calories, salt, and saturated fat, and eating it regularly is linked to weight gain and low energy levels. I definitely notice I feel sluggish after eating too much of it.

As healthier alternatives, I'd suggest keeping simple things at home — like yoghurt with fruit, wholegrain wraps with hummus, or overnight oats. They're just as quick to prepare but much better for you.

Hope this helps with your project!
Best wishes,
Alex
